metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a concrete SIGTERM drain deadline value (e.g., 35 min) and require a matching `stop_grace_period` (e.g., 36 min) in the compose service definition for the autolander, ensuring Docker does not SIGKILL the process before the bot's internal deadline allows it to write recovery.json.
findings.finding: The drain deadline is described as '~ the CI-wait timeout' but the plan never names a concrete duration or specifies that Docker's `stop_grace_period` must be set to accommodate it. Docker's default `stop_grace_period` is 10 seconds — far shorter than a CI-wait timeout (typically minutes to tens of minutes). Without specifying `stop_grace_period` in the compose service definition, Docker will SIGKILL the process after 10 s regardless of the bot's internal deadline, making the drain-and-write path unreachable in practice for any non-trivial CI-wait duration.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — 'drain is time-bounded (SIGTERM deadline ~ the CI-wait timeout)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the docker-compose.yml autolander service spec: `stop_grace_period: 36m` (or whatever the CI-wait timeout + margin is). Add to the drain AC: 'SIGTERM deadline = CI-wait timeout + 60 s; stop_grace_period = SIGTERM deadline + 60 s.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the crash-recovery test AC whether Gerrit and S3 calls use live endpoints or faithful test doubles, and — if mocks are used for the `phase=submitting` reconciliation — add an explicit live/integration acceptance criterion (or name a child ticket) that exercises the real Gerrit status-check and re-drive path end-to-end.
findings.finding: The crash-recovery/idempotency test is silent on whether it exercises LIVE Gerrit and S3 or uses mocks/stubs. The `phase=submitting` reconciliation decision ('check LIVE Gerrit: all members MERGED → write merged outcome; not merged → re-drive') is the highest-risk path; if the test uses a mock Gerrit that short-circuits the live status-check, the actual live-reconciliation code path is never exercised end-to-end, and the suite can pass while the live path is broken.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Crash-recovery/idempotency test AC; recovery decision rule for `phase=submitting`
findings.suggested_fix: Amend the test AC to read: 'The `phase=submitting` kill-and-restart case must be covered by an integration test that queries a real (or real-equivalent seam) Gerrit instance, or a named follow-on ticket must track the live end-to-end validation of the submitting-phase reconciliation path.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete SIGTERM drain deadline value (e.g. derived from actual CI-wait p99), set `stop_grace_period` in docker-compose to at least that value, and add a test case that exercises the deadline-exceeded path (bot is killed before drain completes) to confirm recovery.json is written and restart reconciles correctly.
findings.finding: The SIGTERM drain deadline is described as '~ the CI-wait timeout' — asserted and vague, with no measurement plan and no Docker/compose stop_grace_period configuration specified. If the orchestrator's SIGKILL arrives before the drain deadline, recovery.json is never written and the crash-safe guarantee silently breaks. The crash-recovery idempotency test does not appear to exercise the deadline-exceeded path specifically.
findings.location: AC: 'Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery' — 'the drain is time-bounded (SIGTERM deadline ~ the CI-wait timeout)'
findings.suggested_fix: In `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` or the compose file, set `stop_grace_period` to `<CI_WAIT_TIMEOUT + buffer>`. In the plan, state the concrete deadline (e.g. 'drain deadline = CI_WAIT_TIMEOUT − 30 s to leave time for recovery.json write'). Add a test case: inject a fake slow-CI hang, send SIGTERM, assert recovery.json is written within the deadline even when CI has not finished.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the HEALTHCHECK staleness threshold explicitly in AC4 (e.g., 'HEALTHCHECK marks unhealthy if heartbeat file age > 90 s, matching the lander_down threshold in AC5').
findings.finding: The HEALTHCHECK staleness threshold is referenced by name ('unhealthy if older than the staleness threshold') but never defined numerically. AC5 defines two heartbeat thresholds (freshness ≤15 s; lander_down >90 s) but neither is explicitly designated as the HEALTHCHECK threshold, leaving the executor to guess.
findings.location: AC4 (Liveness/restart) — 'unhealthy if older than the staleness threshold'
findings.suggested_fix: In AC4, change to: 'unhealthy if the heartbeat file is older than 90 s (the same 6× poll threshold used by lander_down in AC5)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the HEALTHCHECK staleness threshold explicitly in AC4 (e.g. 'unhealthy if heartbeat file is older than 90 s, matching the lander_down boundary') so AC4 and AC5 reference the same numeric contract.
findings.finding: The HEALTHCHECK staleness threshold (AC4: 'unhealthy if older than the staleness threshold') is never given a concrete value in the plan, while AC5 defines 90 s only for `lander_down`. If the implementer picks a different value for the HEALTHCHECK (e.g. 30 s or 60 s), the HEALTHCHECK could restart the bot before `lander_down` triggers (or vice versa), undermining the layered-reliability design.
findings.location: AC4 (Liveness/restart) and AC5 (Heartbeat)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4: 'the HEALTHCHECK uses the same 90 s threshold as lander_down (unhealthy if heartbeat file age > 90 s), ensuring both liveness layers trip at the same boundary.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a poll-and-wait step in the `phase=submitting` recovery path: after restart, before deciding to re-drive, the bot MUST re-query Gerrit for a bounded window (e.g., up to 30 s with exponential backoff) to allow any in-flight async submit to complete and reveal the true final state (`MERGED` or not), rather than acting immediately on a potentially transient `NEW` status.
findings.finding: The `phase=submitting` recovery path contains a TOCTOU race that breaks the plan's double-submit safety guarantee: after a crash during `POST /submit`, Gerrit may still be processing the submit asynchronously. On restart the bot queries Gerrit, sees status `NEW` (submit enqueued but not yet complete), concludes 'not merged → re-drive,' and issues a second `POST /submit`. The plan's decision rule ('not merged → re-drive') does not account for the window between submit dispatch and Gerrit completing the merge, and specifies no poll/wait before making the re-drive decision.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — `phase=submitting` bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `phase=submitting` recovery bullet: 'On restart: poll Gerrit status for all chain members with exponential backoff (e.g., 2 s, 4 s, 8 s, 16 s, max 60 s total) before deciding; only if status is still not MERGED after the poll window has elapsed, proceed with re-drive. If any member reaches MERGED during the poll window, treat as a completed submit and write the merged outcome.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a concrete HEALTHCHECK staleness threshold value (e.g., 45 s = 3× the 15 s poll) and a `stop-period` / `start-period` value in the Dockerfile HEALTHCHECK instruction, ensuring the threshold is meaningfully smaller than the 90 s `lander_down` boundary so autoheal can complete a restart cycle before lander_down fires.
findings.finding: The HEALTHCHECK staleness threshold in AC4 is specified only as 'unhealthy if older than the staleness threshold' — no concrete value is given. AC5 gives 90 s for `lander_down`, but the HEALTHCHECK threshold must be a different (smaller) value to ensure autoheal restarts the container before `lander_down` fires downstream. Without a concrete value, the implementer must invent the threshold, and there is no guarantee the chosen value gives autoheal enough time to restart the container before the 90 s `lander_down` boundary.
findings.location: AC4: Liveness/restart — HEALTHCHECK bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4: 'HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=5s --start-period=20s --retries=1 CMD stat the heartbeat file and check age < 45 s (3× poll).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the HEALTHCHECK staleness threshold (the maximum heartbeat age in seconds that is still considered healthy), and the `--interval`, `--timeout`, `--start-period`, and `--retries` values in AC4 or the Dockerfile.autolander spec, so the implementer has unambiguous parameters.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the concrete staleness threshold used in the `HEALTHCHECK` stat command in `Dockerfile.autolander`, nor the HEALTHCHECK `--interval`, `--timeout`, `--start-period`, or `--retries` values. AC4 says 'unhealthy if older than the staleness threshold' but leaves the threshold implicit. The 90 s `lander_down` threshold (AC5) and the 15 s poll cadence suggest the HEALTHCHECK threshold should be somewhere between these, but the Dockerfile author has no authoritative value to implement against.
findings.location: AC4 (Liveness/restart) — Dockerfile.autolander HEALTHCHECK
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4: 'HEALTHCHECK parameters: `--interval=30s --timeout=5s --start-period=60s --retries=3`; the stat check uses a staleness threshold of **60 s** (4× poll, between the 15 s write cadence and the 90 s lander_down threshold) so autoheal triggers before `land` declares the bot down but tolerates transient Gerrit-call delays.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `handback` (or equivalent) value to the `phase` enum in S2b's schema and specify the `phase` value written to recovery.json when the bot is killed during handback, so S5d's recovery dispatch rule is executable.
findings.finding: 'mid-handback' is described as a kill-point phase in the recovery decision table but has no corresponding value in the S2b-defined phase enum `{selecting, rebasing, awaiting_verified, submitting, idle, paused}`. The plan therefore cannot specify what `phase` value is written to recovery.json when the bot is killed during handback, making the recovery dispatch rule unexecutable.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — 'mid-handback' bullet; S2b AC1 (`wipChain` schema)
findings.suggested_fix: In S2b AC1, extend the enum to `phase ∈ {selecting, rebasing, awaiting_verified, submitting, handback, idle, paused}`. In S5d, update the recovery bullet to read 'phase=handback — complete the idempotent Autosubmit-removal ...' so the dispatch condition maps 1-to-1 with a writeable enum value.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete drain deadline in the plan (e.g., 'SIGTERM drain timeout = the CI-wait timeout constant from S2 config, currently X minutes; cap at Y seconds if that constant is unavailable at shutdown') so the executor has an actionable number.
findings.finding: The SIGTERM drain deadline is specified only as '~ the CI-wait timeout' — an approximation that does not give the executor a concrete value to code against. If the CI-wait timeout lives in a config constant the executor must discover, this blocks implementation without clarification.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — 'SIGTERM deadline ~ the CI-wait timeout'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: 'The SIGTERM drain deadline equals the CI-wait timeout (e.g. 20 min per S2 config key `CI_WAIT_TIMEOUT_S`); if the deadline elapses, write recovery.json and exit immediately.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `recovery.json` is written atomically (write to a sibling tmpfile, then `os.replace()` / rename into place) so a mid-write SIGKILL leaves either the old complete file or the new complete file — never a partial — and add an explicit decision rule for an absent/corrupt `recovery.json` on restart (e.g., treat as absent → re-select from scratch, with a logged warning).
findings.finding: `recovery.json` write atomicity is unspecified, and the plan provides no decision rule or back-out path for a corrupt/partial file on restart — a SIGKILL mid-write (Docker hard-kills after stop_grace_period) leaves an unreadable record, which silently strands the in-flight wipChain.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — '…writes a concrete recovery record (`recovery.json` in the state volume…) and exits rather than blocking the redeploy. On restart the bot reads `recovery.json`…'
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC, add: 'The write MUST be atomic: write full JSON to `recovery.json.tmp`, then `os.replace("recovery.json.tmp", "recovery.json")`. On restart, if `recovery.json` is absent or fails JSON parsing, log a warning and treat as no in-flight work (re-select from scratch); delete any leftover `.tmp` file.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a timeout and retry/backoff policy for the Gerrit and S3 calls made during startup reconciliation, and define the fallback: either surface a startup-failed health status (not heartbeat) or fall back to a safe re-select-from-scratch after N failed attempts.
findings.finding: The restart reconciliation unconditionally reads LIVE Gerrit and S3 with no specified timeout, circuit-breaker, or fallback — if either dependency is down at restart time, the bot hangs in reconciliation and never begins processing, with no health signal distinguishing 'reconciling' from 'stuck'.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — 'On restart the bot reads recovery.json + reconciles against LIVE Gerrit and S3's markers BEFORE selecting new work'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the `phase=submitting` recovery rule that the `re_drive_count` from recovery.json is preserved (additive against the N=5 cap) — not reset — so the bound holds across restarts.
findings.finding: The `re_drive_count` field is preserved in the recovery.json snapshot and carried into the restarted instance, but the `phase=submitting` recovery rule ('re-drive: re-verify FFO-landability, re-await, re-submit') never specifies whether the restored `re_drive_count` is additive against the N=5 cap (correct) or silently reset to 0 (which would allow up to 5 additional re-drives, defeating the bounded-re-drive guarantee).
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — `phase=submitting` bullet and `re_drive_count` field in recovery.json schema
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the submitting recovery bullet: 'Restore `re_drive_count` from the snapshot; the N=5 cap continues to apply — count is not reset on restart.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan: (a) state how the bot behaves when recovery.json is absent (treat as clean start); (b) describe how to revert to pre-drain SIGTERM behavior if the drain causes a stuck redeploy (e.g. `docker stop --time` override, or a feature flag).
findings.finding: The plan changes SIGTERM semantics from (presumably) immediate exit to a time-bounded drain, and introduces recovery.json as a new persistent data shape on the state volume, but provides no rollback/back-out plan if the drain causes operational problems (e.g. a stuck wipChain blocks redeploy past the deadline) or if recovery.json is malformed/absent on first boot under the new code.
findings.location: AC: 'Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery' section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving step for AC4: freeze the heartbeat file (e.g. `chmod -w heartbeat.ts`), wait > threshold seconds, then assert `docker inspect --format '{{.State.Health.Status}}' autolander | grep -q unhealthy`.
findings.finding: No concrete proving command exists for AC4's HEALTHCHECK behavior — the plan describes the intended outcome (container marked unhealthy after heartbeat staleness) but provides no docker-level verification step.
findings.location: AC4 (Liveness/restart) — HEALTHCHECK correctness

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving step for the emergency-stop AC: create the sentinel file while the bot is running, then assert that within one poll cycle the status endpoint reports `phase=paused` and no new changes are dequeued (e.g., via log assertion or S3 marker count).
findings.finding: The emergency-stop AC has no proving command — the plan specifies the sentinel-file mechanism and the expected phase transition to 'paused' but provides no step or test to verify the bot actually stops consuming new Autosubmit changes when the file is present.
findings.location: AC: Emergency-stop (prior art: Zuul pause)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the test file (e.g. `tests/test_crash_recovery.py` or `infra/autolander/tests/test_recovery.py`) to the Files section with a one-line description of what it exercises.
findings.finding: The crash-recovery/idempotency test AC ('an automated test kills the bot at each phase … and asserts …') is an explicit deliverable with no corresponding test file in the Files section, making it an orphan criterion.
findings.location: Last AC bullet ('Crash-recovery/idempotency test') vs. Files section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Files: `tests/autolander/test_crash_recovery.py` — NEW: parameterised kill-point tests for each phase (rebasing, awaiting_verified, submitting, mid-handback); asserts no double-submit and S3 marker converges to acknowledged=true.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that recovery.json is written atomically (write to a `.tmp` sibling, fsync, then rename) so a SIGKILL mid-write leaves either the old complete file or no file, never a corrupt one; also specify the restart behavior when the file is absent (treat as clean start).
findings.finding: recovery.json write atomicity is unspecified: if the process receives SIGKILL (from Docker after the SIGTERM deadline elapses), a non-atomic write leaves a partially written or zero-length recovery.json that the restarted bot will attempt to parse, potentially corrupting reconciliation.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — 'writes a concrete recovery record (recovery.json in the state volume)'
findings.suggested_fix: Write to `recovery.json.tmp`, call `fsync`, then `os.replace('recovery.json.tmp', 'recovery.json')`. On startup, if only `.tmp` exists (rename never completed) treat as absent and do a clean re-select.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that recovery.json is deleted (or atomically renamed to recovery.json.done) immediately after successful reconciliation on restart, so subsequent clean restarts do not re-trigger reconciliation against stale state.
findings.finding: The recovery.json file has no defined RETIRE/cleanup path: after a successful restart reconciliation, the plan does not specify that recovery.json is deleted, so every subsequent restart will re-attempt reconciliation against now-stale data from the previous incident.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — 'On restart the bot reads recovery.json + reconciles…'
findings.suggested_fix: After reconciliation completes and the decision rule has been applied, call `os.unlink('recovery.json')` inside the same logical transaction (before entering the main poll loop).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add instrumentation requirements for: (a) log at WARN when drain timeout is hit and recovery.json is written (include phase and change_id); (b) log at INFO for each reconciliation decision applied on restart; (c) log at WARN when emergency-stop sentinel is detected (and when it is cleared); consider a `recovery_events_total` counter metric.
findings.finding: Key operational events introduced by this plan — drain timeout being hit, recovery.json being written, reconciliation outcome (which decision rule was applied, and why), and emergency-stop being toggled — are not specified to emit any log, metric, or alert, leaving operators blind to whether the reliability fallback is functioning.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery; AC: Emergency-stop sentinel

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test (or explicit test AC) for the emergency-stop sentinel: assert that creating the sentinel file transitions the bot to `phase=paused` (stops consuming new Autosubmit changes) and that removing it resumes normal operation.
findings.finding: The emergency-stop sentinel — a new operator-facing control (create file → bot pauses; remove file → bot resumes) — has no test specified anywhere in the plan. Both the transition from running→paused on sentinel presence and the resume from paused→running on sentinel removal are untested new user-facing behaviors.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Emergency-stop (prior art: Zuul `pause`) AC
findings.suggested_fix: Add a new AC: 'Emergency-stop test: an automated test (a) creates the sentinel file while the bot is mid-poll and asserts it enters `paused` phase and skips new work; (b) removes the sentinel and asserts the bot returns to active polling within one cycle.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `Dockerfile.autolander` is created by S5a and add an explicit dependency on S5a landing first, OR relabel this entry CREATE and include the minimal Dockerfile stub content this ticket must originate.
findings.finding: `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` is labeled EDIT in the Files section, implying the file already exists, but no such file is present in `infra/compose/` — only `Dockerfile.gerrit`, `Dockerfile.reviewbot`, `docker-compose.yml`, and `gerrit.config` exist there.
findings.location: ## Files — `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander — EDIT: the HEALTHCHECK`
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'Depends on: S5a (08e1-5505-d898-4192) — creates Dockerfile.autolander' in the plan header, and note the EDIT label is conditional on S5a having merged.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit prerequisite block naming S5a (08e1-5505-d898-4192) as a hard dependency for AC4, and confirm the autoheal sidecar + autoheal=true label are verified as delivered before this ticket is executed.
findings.finding: AC4 asserts `the willfarrell/autoheal sidecar (S5a)` restarts the `autoheal=true`-labeled container, implying S5a already added the autoheal service and label to docker-compose.yml — but the live `docker-compose.yml` contains only `gerrit` and `review-bot` services with no autolander service, no autoheal sidecar, and no `autoheal=true` label anywhere.
findings.location: AC4 — Liveness/restart: 'The willfarrell/autoheal sidecar (S5a) restarts the autoheal=true-labeled container on unhealthy'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `depends_on: S5a (08e1-5505-d898-4192)` to S5d's deps (or promote the label to `NEW/EDIT: add HEALTHCHECK` with a note that S5a creates the base file) so the executor knows Dockerfile.autolander must exist before this edit.
findings.finding: `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` is labeled EDIT in S5d's Files section but is created NEW only by sibling task S5a (08e1-5505-d898-4192). S5d declares no dep on S5a, meaning the EDIT target may not exist when S5d executes.
findings.location: ## Files — `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` — EDIT: the `HEALTHCHECK`
findings.suggested_fix: Add `depends_on: ['08e1-5505-d898-4192']` to S5d's ticket deps, and change the Files label to `EDIT (created by S5a)` to make the sequencing requirement explicit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Require that `recovery.json` is written atomically (write to a `.recovery.json.tmp` sibling, then `os.rename` to `recovery.json`) so a mid-write SIGKILL leaves either a complete previous version or no file — never a partial/corrupt file.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that the bot writes `recovery.json` before exiting past the SIGTERM deadline, but does not require the write to be atomic (write-to-temp + `os.rename`). If Docker issues a post-grace-period SIGKILL while the write is in progress, `recovery.json` is left partially written or zero-length. On restart the bot reads a corrupt file; the plan specifies no handling for this case, leaving the restart in an undefined state — exactly the scenario the reliability fallback is designed to handle.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — recovery.json write paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: In the recovery.json write spec, add: 'Write atomically: serialize to `recovery.json.tmp` then `os.rename("recovery.json.tmp", "recovery.json")` — rename is atomic on POSIX local filesystems, so a SIGKILL mid-write leaves the previous complete version intact.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a retirement step for `recovery.json`: after the bot successfully completes restart reconciliation and resumes normal operation, it MUST delete (or rename to `recovery.json.done`) the file so subsequent restarts start clean.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that on restart the bot reads `recovery.json` and reconciles, but never specifies when or whether `recovery.json` is retired (deleted or invalidated) after successful reconciliation. If the file persists, every subsequent bot restart — including planned redeploys with no prior crash — will re-run the recovery reconciliation against stale data, potentially misidentifying a clean restart as a crash recovery.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — 'On restart the bot reads recovery.json' paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the restart reconciliation spec: 'After reconciliation completes (all phases resolved and S3 marker converged to acknowledged=true), the bot deletes recovery.json before entering the normal selection loop.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit **Depends on S5a** prerequisite note in S5d's plan stating that the autolander state volume (compose-up.sh SITE_SUBDIRS extension + docker-compose.yml `external: true` volume declaration) must be provisioned before S5d's runtime paths (heartbeat file, recovery.json, sentinel) will function correctly.
findings.finding: S5d's `## Files` section omits the compose-up.sh SITE_SUBDIRS extension and docker-compose.yml edits that provision the autolander state volume — the volume that AC5/AC4/drain-on-shutdown all write to (heartbeat file, recovery.json, sentinel). These are assigned to S5a in the parent story. S5d's own plan text doesn't call out this hard prerequisite, so an implementer working from S5d alone has no signal that the state volume must pre-exist before loop.py writes will succeed.
findings.location: ## Files section and AC5/drain-on-shutdown AC
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `## Prerequisites` or `## Dependencies` section to S5d: 'Requires S5a to have extended SITE_SUBDIRS in compose-up.sh and declared the autolander state volume as `external: true` in docker-compose.yml; S5d's loop.py writes (heartbeat, recovery.json, sentinel) target that volume and will silently go to the ephemeral container layer if it is absent.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin `willfarrell/autoheal` to a specific digest or semver tag in docker-compose.yml, and state the auth/mirror precondition (Docker Hub credentials in `.env` or an ECR/private mirror) required to pull it in the deployment runbook or S5a AC.
findings.finding: The `willfarrell/autoheal` image is referenced with no version tag, implying `:latest`. The existing compose stack uses only locally-built images (all services use `build: context`); this is the first external image pull from Docker Hub in the stack. No auth precondition, registry mirror, or Docker Hub rate-limit handling is stated. An EC2 instance hitting Docker Hub anonymous pull limits during a redeploy will fail to start the autoheal sidecar, silently disabling the liveness restart mechanism.
findings.location: AC4 (Liveness/restart) — 'The `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar (S5a)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `image: willfarrell/autoheal:v1.2.0` (or the current stable tag) in the compose service definition, and add a `DOCKER_REGISTRY_USER` / `DOCKER_REGISTRY_PASS` note to `fetch-secrets.sh` or document a registry mirror so the pull is not rate-limited.

findings.finding: No proving command or test is specified for the HEALTHCHECK behavior (AC4) or the emergency-stop transition (AC3); an agent cannot verify these criteria are met beyond reading the code.
findings.location: AC4 (Liveness/restart) and AC3 (Emergency-stop)

findings.finding: The plan claims 'crash-safe recovery' in both the title and the AC, but the recovery.json mechanism is only reachable via SIGTERM handling. True crashes — OOM kills (SIGKILL from the kernel), host reboots, or container runtime force-kills — bypass the SIGTERM handler entirely, leave no recovery.json, and leave the bot in whatever mid-phase state it was in. The crash-recovery test AC only says 'kills the bot at each phase' without mandating SIGKILL-level kill signals, so the test does not actually validate the title's crash-safe promise.
findings.location: Title; AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery; AC: Crash-recovery/idempotency test

findings.finding: The last AC mandates 'an automated test kills the bot at each phase … and asserts the restarted instance neither double-submits nor strands a stack' but no test file appears in the Files section, leaving the criterion with no corresponding described deliverable.
findings.location: AC: 'Crash-recovery/idempotency test' and '## Files' section
findings.suggested_fix: Append to Files: `tests/autolander/test_crash_recovery.py — ADD: parameterized kill-at-phase tests (rebasing, awaiting_verified, submitting, mid-handback); assert no double-submit and acknowledged=true after restart.'

findings.finding: `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` is labeled 'EDIT' in the Files section — implying it already exists — but the file is absent from the repo: `infra/compose/` contains only `Dockerfile.gerrit`, `Dockerfile.reviewbot`, `docker-compose.yml`, and `gerrit.config`.
findings.location: ## Files — `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` — EDIT

findings.finding: `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` is labeled EDIT in S5d's Files section but does not exist in the repo and is created as NEW only by sibling task S5a (`08e1-5505-d898-4192`). S5d has no `depends_on` edge to S5a — they are sibling tasks under the same parent S5, whose only `depends_on` is S2. The rubric's recursive-depends_on exception does not apply. If S5d lands before S5a, the HEALTHCHECK edit target is absent.
findings.location: Files section: `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander — EDIT: the HEALTHCHECK`
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add `depends_on: 08e1-5505-d898-4192` to S5d so the ordering is enforced, or (b) move the HEALTHCHECK line into S5a's Dockerfile.autolander creation (with a placeholder `CMD stat ...` that S5d's loop.py heartbeat write path makes functional), removing the cross-sibling file dependency entirely.

findings.finding: The emergency-stop AC (AC3) introduces a new operator-facing flow — sentinel file presence transitions the bot to `paused` and halts consumption — but no test is specified anywhere in the plan for this behavior.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 (Emergency-stop sentinel)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC5 or add a dedicated AC6: 'an automated test creates the sentinel file mid-poll, asserts the bot enters `paused` (no new work picked up), removes the sentinel, and asserts the bot resumes within one poll cycle.'

findings.finding: The drain time-bound path — 'past the deadline it writes a concrete recovery record and exits rather than blocking the redeploy' — is a new failure-mode behavior with no test covering the deadline-exceeded scenario.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 (Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery), deadline sub-clause

findings.finding: recovery.json has no defined RETIRE/cleanup path after a successful recovery reconciliation. If the file is not deleted (or overwritten) after the bot successfully reconciles, every subsequent restart will re-read it and attempt redundant re-reconciliation, risking spurious re-drives or mis-classification of already-acknowledged stacks.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — recovery.json lifecycle
findings.suggested_fix: After reconciliation completes successfully (all stacks resolved, S3 markers converged), atomically rename recovery.json to recovery.json.prev (or delete it). On startup, treat absence of recovery.json as 'clean start'; treat presence as 'needs reconciliation'. Add this lifecycle step explicitly to the AC.

findings.finding: `recovery.json` has no specified retirement (delete or overwrite-to-empty) path after a successful reconciliation. Every subsequent clean restart will read the stale file and re-enter the recovery branch, causing spurious Gerrit + S3 reconciliation calls even for a bot that started cleanly after a graceful stop.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — 'On restart the bot reads recovery.json + reconciles against LIVE Gerrit and S3's markers BEFORE selecting new work'
findings.suggested_fix: After the reconciliation loop completes without error, `os.unlink(recovery_path)` (or `recovery_path.rename(recovery_path.with_suffix('.done'))` for audit trail). If reconciliation raises an unrecoverable error, leave the file in place and exit so the next restart can retry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command for AC5's status-endpoint claim, e.g.: `curl -s http://localhost:PORT/status | jq -e '.heartbeat_age_s | . >= 0 and . <= 15'` run immediately after a fresh poll cycle.
findings.finding: No concrete proving command exists for AC5 (heartbeat_age_s field served by S5c's status endpoint). The AC claims 'its age is served as heartbeat_age_s by S5c's status endpoint' but no step or check verifies the field appears in the response with a correct value.
findings.location: AC5 (Heartbeat) — 'its age is served as heartbeat_age_s by S5c's status endpoint'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expose `HEARTBEAT_POLL_INTERVAL_S` (default 15) and `LANDER_DOWN_STALENESS_S` (default 90) as environment variables (or entries in the existing config module) so operators can tune them without code changes.
findings.finding: The 90 s staleness threshold for `lander_down` and the 15 s poll cadence are hardcoded in the plan with no mention of making them configurable (env vars or config file). These are operational thresholds that site-reliability concerns will drive to change (e.g. slower CI, different deployment environments).
findings.location: AC5 (Heartbeat): 'freshness ≤ 15 s; land's lander_down treats age > 90 s (6× poll) as down'
findings.suggested_fix: In loop.py: `POLL_INTERVAL = int(os.environ.get('HEARTBEAT_POLL_INTERVAL_S', 15))` and `LANDER_DOWN_STALENESS = int(os.environ.get('LANDER_DOWN_STALENESS_S', 90))`. Reference these constants in the HEALTHCHECK CMD.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `docs/adr/NNN-crash-safe-recovery.md` (or CLAUDE.md section) capturing the per-phase decision rules and recovery.json contract, and add a `schema_version` field to recovery.json so the reconciliation code can detect and handle stale or future-format files.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern (crash-safe recovery via recovery.json with per-phase decision rules) and a new cross-component state contract (recovery.json schema), but includes no mention of capturing this in an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design document. The recovery.json schema also lacks a version field, risking silent misbehavior if the schema evolves between deployments.
findings.location: AC: 'Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery' — recovery.json schema definition; Files section (no ADR/doc file listed)
findings.suggested_fix: recovery.json: add `"schema_version": 1` as the first field. In reconciliation code: if `schema_version` is missing or mismatched, log a warning and discard (treat as no recovery file), then proceed with fresh selection. Create `docs/adr/NNN-crash-safe-recovery.md` listing the four-phase decision table.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether `land`'s `lander_down` already reads `heartbeat_age_s` from S5c and applies the >90 s threshold, OR add the relevant `land` file to the Files section and specify the code change required.
findings.finding: The Files section lists only `loop.py` and `Dockerfile.autolander`, but AC5 specifies that `land`'s `lander_down` function 'treats age > 90 s as down' — if this threshold check requires a code change in `land`, that caller file is absent from scope and the AC would not be fully satisfied by the listed files alone.
findings.location: AC5 / Files section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to AC5: 'No change to `land` is required — `lander_down` was updated in S5c to consume `heartbeat_age_s` from the status endpoint.' OR add `infra/autolander/land.py` (or equivalent) to the Files section with the edit description 'update `lander_down` to read `heartbeat_age_s` and return `True` when age > 90 s'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider (advisory) whether the emergency-stop sentinel AC is independently shippable ahead of the drain+crash-safe-recovery work, given it has no coupling to recovery.json or the kill-point test harness.
findings.finding: The unit bundles three concerns that could be independently released: (1) heartbeat + autoheal, (2) emergency-stop sentinel, and (3) drain + crash-safe recovery — each has a distinct reason to change (infra reliability, operator control, data-safety respectively) and each would be independently testable and valuable. While autoheal and crash-safe recovery share ordering dependency, the emergency-stop concern is fully orthogonal.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all ACs) / Why section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `willfarrell/autoheal` is necessary rather than a HEALTHCHECK script that terminates PID 1 on heartbeat staleness (which would let the existing `restart: unless-stopped` pattern handle recovery without a new sidecar or Docker socket mount).
findings.finding: The plan introduces `willfarrell/autoheal` as a new third-party sidecar (with Docker socket mount) to restart the autolander on HEALTHCHECK `unhealthy`, but every other service in the compose stack uses `restart: unless-stopped` (docker-compose.yml lines 34 and 80). A simpler in-stack alternative exists: a HEALTHCHECK script that sends SIGTERM to PID 1 (or exits the process) when the heartbeat file is stale would allow Docker's native `restart: unless-stopped` to handle recovery without a new sidecar image or Docker socket exposure. The plan does not acknowledge or reject this alternative.
findings.location: AC4 (Liveness/restart) — 'The `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar (S5a) restarts the `autoheal=true`-labeled container on `unhealthy`.'
findings.suggested_fix: If the autolander process can be structured so its HEALTHCHECK script sends SIGTERM to PID 1 (or calls `kill 1`) on stale heartbeat, then `restart: unless-stopped` handles the restart natively — matching the established stack pattern and eliminating the autoheal sidecar dependency. If the sidecar is required (e.g., because the unhealthy container must not self-terminate mid-drain), add a one-sentence rationale to the plan explaining why the native approach is insufficient.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `infra/autolander/loop.py` (and its parent directory) is created by a predecessor ticket (e.g., S2a) and add an explicit dependency note, OR relabel this entry CREATE if it is this ticket's responsibility to originate the file.
findings.finding: `infra/autolander/loop.py` is labeled EDIT in the Files section — implying the file already exists — but the directory `infra/autolander/` does not exist anywhere in the repo. The plan's entire edit-based approach for adding heartbeat writes, emergency-stop sentinel checks, SIGTERM drain, and restart-recovery reconciliation rests on a pre-existing file that cannot be confirmed.
findings.location: ## Files — `infra/autolander/loop.py — EDIT`
findings.suggested_fix: Change `infra/autolander/loop.py — EDIT` to `infra/autolander/loop.py — EDIT (created by S2a; depends on S2a landing first)` and add an explicit prerequisite block listing S2a as a hard dependency.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` as an unenumerated consumer: note that S5a/S5d's compose and Dockerfile changes affect its `BOT_PATHS` trigger logic, and either add `Dockerfile.autolander` to a separate `AUTOLANDER_PATHS` guard or acknowledge that autolander rebuilds are manual.
findings.finding: `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh`'s `BOT_PATHS` variable (line 55) hardcodes `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` but not `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander`. When S5a adds `Dockerfile.autolander` changes land, autodeploy will NOT rebuild autolander on Dockerfile-only changes — and when docker-compose.yml is changed (S5a), autodeploy will incorrectly trigger a review-bot rebuild. This unenumerated consumer is not acknowledged by S5d.
findings.location: ## Files — `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` — EDIT

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge `infra/scripts/observability.sh` (S5c's HEALTHCHECK feeder) as a downstream consumer of S5d's HEALTHCHECK output, and document the deploy ordering requirement (S5d must land before or simultaneously with S5c's feeder).
findings.finding: `infra/scripts/observability.sh` is an unenumerated consumer of S5d's HEALTHCHECK deliverable: S5c's plan (ticket 9a6f-065d-91c4-4e03) adds a `docker inspect --format '{{.State.Health.Status}}'` feeder to observability.sh that depends on the HEALTHCHECK this plan writes to `Dockerfile.autolander`. Without S5d's HEALTHCHECK, S5c's feeder returns `<no value>`, silently corrupting the `autolander_errors` metric. S5d does not acknowledge observability.sh as a downstream consumer.
findings.location: AC: Liveness/restart (AC4) — HEALTHCHECK

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale for the sentinel-file-in-state-volume approach to the emergency-stop AC (e.g., 'chosen because it requires no tooling beyond touch/rm, is readable without an additional dependency in loop.py, and survives both container replacement and process restart within the same container').
findings.finding: The plan provides a negative rationale for the emergency-stop sentinel design ('Lives on the state volume so a redeploy does not silently un-pause') but no positive rationale for WHY the sentinel-file-in-state-volume approach was chosen over alternatives (e.g., a Gerrit project-level label vote that the bot reads each poll, or an API endpoint write). The absence of a positive rationale leaves the implementer without grounding for evaluating trade-offs or deviating in edge cases.
findings.location: AC: Emergency-stop (prior art: Zuul `pause`) bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (via autoheal docs or empirical test) whether mounting the Docker socket `:ro` silently disables `POST /containers/{id}/restart` calls; if so, remove the `:ro` flag from the autoheal socket mount or document why it is safe.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `willfarrell/autoheal` with the Docker socket mounted `:ro`, but autoheal's core restart function requires issuing `POST /containers/{id}/restart` against the Docker daemon API — a write operation. Whether Docker's `:ro` flag on a Unix socket mount silently disables write-path API calls (making restarts fail silently) or is merely a no-op mount-point flag is an unverified capability assertion that is not resolved in the plan.
findings.location: AC4 (Liveness/restart) — 'willfarrell/autoheal sidecar (S5a) restarts the autoheal=true-labeled container'; S5a Files: 'willfarrell/autoheal sidecar (Docker socket :ro)'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `:ro` from the Docker socket mount for the autoheal sidecar (or confirm via Docker engine source/docs that unix socket `:ro` does not restrict API method verbs) and add a test AC that autoheal actually restarts an unhealthy container.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit justification in the plan for why `willfarrell/autoheal` is needed rather than Docker's native `restart: unless-stopped` (e.g., 'Docker does not restart containers on HEALTHCHECK=unhealthy unless they also exit; autoheal bridges this gap').
findings.finding: The plan introduces `willfarrell/autoheal` as a new sidecar dependency but does not consider Docker's native `restart: unless-stopped` + HEALTHCHECK behavior, which is already the established pattern for every other service in the compose stack. Docker's own daemon will restart an unhealthy container when `restart: unless-stopped` is set — making the autoheal sidecar potentially redundant with an established in-stack mechanism.
findings.location: AC4 (Liveness/restart) — 'willfarrell/autoheal sidecar (S5a) restarts the autoheal=true-labeled container'; infra/compose/docker-compose.yml (existing pattern: `restart: unless-stopped` for gerrit and review-bot)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-sentence rationale: 'Docker's native restart policy only fires on container exit, not on HEALTHCHECK=unhealthy; `willfarrell/autoheal` is needed to restart a hanging (non-exiting) container that the HEALTHCHECK marks unhealthy.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and document that `willfarrell/autoheal` can issue `POST /containers/{id}/restart` with the Docker socket mounted `:ro`; if `:ro` is intended as a security posture, confirm it does not block the restart write-call, and add an explicit note or test in AC4 asserting the sidecar successfully restarts an unhealthy autolander container.
findings.finding: The `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar (introduced in S5a, consumed by S5d's AC4) is configured with the Docker socket mounted `:ro` (read-only), but autoheal's sole restart function issues `POST /containers/{id}/restart` against the Docker daemon — a write-protocol operation. On a Unix socket, Docker's `:ro` volume flag governs filesystem mutability of the mount point, not protocol-level writes, so in practice the call may still succeed; however, the plan describes the mount as 'read-only' which (a) creates implementer confusion about whether the sidecar can perform its function, and (b) has been flagged in every prior-review pass on sibling tickets (S5a, S5, parent story) as a capability gap. The plan does not verify or acknowledge this surface-level contradiction, leaving the restart mechanism — the entire liveness guarantee — on an unverified capability claim.
findings.location: AC4 (Liveness/restart) — 'The `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar (S5a) restarts the `autoheal=true`-labeled container on `unhealthy`'
findings.suggested_fix: Either confirm via a local `docker run` test that `willfarrell/autoheal` restarts an `unhealthy` container when the socket is mounted `:ro` and document the result, or change the mount to `:rw` (without the `:ro` flag) with a security-rationale note explaining the accepted socket-exposure trade-off, consistent with how Docker recommends mounting the socket for management sidecars.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify which is the authoritative source for the `acknowledged` flag during restart reconciliation (S3 marker vs recovery.json), and add a rule for the inconsistent case (S3=true, recovery.json=false).
findings.finding: The `acknowledged` flag appears both as a field in recovery.json ('the handback `acknowledged` flag') and as the key for mid-handback recovery ('keyed by S3's `acknowledged=false`'), but the plan never specifies which source is authoritative: recovery.json's own flag or S3's marker. An executor who reads S3 and recovery.json and finds them inconsistent (e.g. recovery.json says acknowledged=false but S3 says acknowledged=true) has no decision rule.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — recovery.json schema definition and mid-handback recovery bullet
findings.suggested_fix: State explicitly: 'On restart for mid-handback, S3's marker is authoritative; if S3 shows acknowledged=true, skip the handback. recovery.json's acknowledged flag is a hint only, used when S3 is unreachable.'

findings.finding: The plan (via its dependency on S5a's docker-compose.yml edit) mounts the Docker socket for the willfarrell/autoheal sidecar as ':ro' (read-only), but autoheal must issue POST /containers/{id}/restart to the Docker daemon — a write syscall — to perform its core restart function. A read-only Unix socket mount silently blocks all write operations, so the autoheal sidecar will be deployed but unable to restart the autolander when it goes unhealthy. This contradicts the asserted capability.
findings.location: AC4 (Liveness/restart) + S5a docker-compose.yml edit: 'willfarrell/autoheal sidecar (Docker socket :ro)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the docker-compose.yml autoheal service definition, mount the socket as `/var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock` (no `:ro`). Document the security implication (socket RW = root-equivalent) inline, consistent with the threat model already accepted for a single-instance Gerrit box.

findings.finding: The crash-recovery reconciliation decision rule is specified for only two of the four named kill-point phases. The plan says: mid-submit → Gerrit check → ack or re-drive; mid-handback → complete idempotent Autosubmit-removal. But AC5 also names `rebasing` and `awaiting_verified` as kill-points. No recovery action is specified for a restart where `recovery.json` contains `phase=rebasing` or `phase=awaiting_verified`. An executor must invent these rules.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery; AC: Crash-recovery/idempotency test

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
